title: Quiz: DRAG Pulses
description: Test your understanding of DRAG pulse techniques for leakage suppression

questions.question: What is leakage error in a transmon qubit?

options.text: Energy loss to the environment (T1 decay)
Population transfer from the computational subspace {|0⟩,|1⟩} to higher levels like |2⟩
Dephasing of the quantum state
Crosstalk between neighboring qubits

questions.explanation: Leakage error occurs when the qubit population coherently transfers out of the computational subspace {|0⟩,|1⟩} into higher energy states, primarily |2⟩. This is distinct from T1 decay (energy loss) or dephasing, and is caused by spectral components of the drive pulse at the 1-2 transition frequency.

questions.question: What does DRAG stand for and what is its primary purpose?

options.text: Derivative Reduction by Amplitude Gating - reduces pulse amplitude
Derivative Removal by Adiabatic Gate - suppresses leakage to |2⟩
Direct Resonance Amplitude Gradient - improves Rabi frequency
Dynamic Rotation And Gating - implements two-qubit gates

questions.explanation: DRAG stands for Derivative Removal by Adiabatic Gate. It suppresses leakage to the |2⟩ state by adding a pulse component proportional to the derivative of the original envelope on the quadrature channel. This creates destructive interference with the leakage pathway.

questions.question: For a DRAG pulse, the quadrature component Q(t) is proportional to:

options.text: The integral of the in-phase component I(t)
The derivative of the in-phase component: Q(t) = β × dI/dt
The square of the in-phase component
A constant offset

questions.explanation: The DRAG quadrature component is proportional to the time derivative of the in-phase envelope: Q(t) = β × dI(t)/dt, where β = -1/α is the DRAG coefficient and α is the anharmonicity. For a Gaussian I(t), this produces an odd (antisymmetric) Q(t).

questions.question: The theoretical DRAG coefficient β depends on the qubit anharmonicity α. For a transmon with α/2π = -250 MHz, what is the approximate theoretical β?

options.text: β ≈ 4 ns (since β = -1/α)
β ≈ 250 ns

questions.explanation: The theoretical DRAG coefficient is β = -1/α. With α = -2π × 250 MHz ≈ -1.57 GHz = -1.57 ns⁻¹, we get β ≈ 0.64 ns. However, in units where α is in MHz and the pulse is in ns, β ≈ -1000/α ≈ 4 ns for α = -250 MHz. The exact value depends on unit conventions.

questions.question: Why does the theoretical DRAG coefficient need experimental calibration?

options.text: The theory is fundamentally incorrect
Real systems have pulse distortions, higher-order nonlinearities, and other effects not captured by first-order theory
The anharmonicity cannot be measured accurately
DRAG only works at specific temperatures

questions.explanation: While first-order DRAG theory provides the correct functional form, the optimal coefficient differs from theory due to: pulse distortions from control electronics, mixer imbalances, higher-order transmon nonlinearities, and AC Stark shifts. Experimental calibration typically finds values within 50% of theory.

questions.question: A common method for DRAG calibration is error amplification using (X, X⁻¹)ⁿ sequences. What is being measured?

options.text: The T1 decay rate
The qubit frequency
Accumulated errors that should cancel for perfect calibration - minimum population deviation indicates optimal β
The readout fidelity

questions.explanation: The (X, X⁻¹)ⁿ sequence should return the qubit to |0⟩ for any n if the gates are perfect. Imperfect DRAG calibration causes accumulated phase errors and leakage that grow with n. By varying β and finding the minimum deviation from |0⟩, the optimal DRAG coefficient is determined.

questions.question: What is a limitation of first-order DRAG pulses?

options.text: They can only be used for X gates, not Y gates
They only address |1⟩→|2⟩ leakage, not higher transitions, and add high-frequency content
They require cryogenic temperatures below 10 mK
They cannot be combined with Gaussian pulse shapes

questions.explanation: First-order DRAG specifically targets the |1⟩→|2⟩ transition and doesn't address leakage to |3⟩ or higher. Additionally, the derivative component adds high-frequency content to the pulse, which can violate the rotating wave approximation for very fast pulses and potentially cause other issues.

questions.question: The √2 factor in the coupling between |1⟩ and |2⟩ in the transmon Hamiltonian means that the 1-2 transition is driven √2 times more strongly than the 0-1 transition for the same field amplitude.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! The transmon behaves approximately as a harmonic oscillator with ladder operators. The matrix element ⟨2|â†|1⟩ = √2 while ⟨1|â†|0⟩ = 1. This means the 1-2 transition couples √2 times more strongly to the drive field, making leakage suppression even more critical.
